key_insights.insight: The 'calories in, calories out' model is an oversimplification of weight loss because it doesn't account for hormonal responses to food.
key_insights.quote: This idea that just eat fewer calories will automatically lead to weight loss is completely false because we know that eating fewer calories leads also to burning fewer calories so you eat 500 less your body but burns 500 less and you're not losing any body weight

key_insights.insight: Eating frequently, especially high-carbohydrate foods, keeps insulin levels elevated, preventing the body from burning stored fat.
key_insights.quote: So now you eat 1,500 calories, but you're keeping your insulin levels really high... your body can only burn 1,500 calories. Your metabolic rate has just now gone down by 500 calories. And guess what? You're not losing any body fat.

key_insights.insight: Yo-yo dieting is detrimental because it can lower your metabolism over time.
key_insights.quote: This form of dieting is actually over the long term probably going to make you gain weight. Oh, it's very detrimental.

key_insights.insight: Intermittent fasting allows insulin levels to fall, enabling the body to access and burn stored fat.
key_insights.quote: Insulin is a hormone that goes up when you eat. It goes down when you don't eat. So when you eat, insulin goes up. Your body wants to store energy. When you don't eat, insulin goes down. Your body says, I have no energy. I have no food coming in. please take it out of storage.

key_insights.insight: Exercise alone is often ineffective for weight loss due to the relatively small number of calories burned and the tendency to eat more afterward.
key_insights.quote: But in terms of weight loss, it's actually a very, very small effect. Why? Because one, the amount of calories you burn during exercise are simply not that high.

key_insights.insight: The key to sustainable weight management lies in controlling the hormonal response to food, not just restricting calories.
key_insights.quote: Food contains calories, energy, but it contains instructions as to what to do. do.

key_insights.insight: Snacking has become institutionalized, leading to more frequent eating and elevated insulin levels.
key_insights.quote: Then it becomes institutionalized as something that every single one of us should be doing. And we should never be without food for more than an hour and a half.

key_insights.insight: Fasting does not necessarily lower your basal metabolic rate; in fact, it can increase it.
key_insights.quote: On day four of zero food, you don't eat any food for four days. They measure how much calories you're burning. Your body is burning 2,200 calories. Your basal metabolic rate didn't go down. It went up.

actionable_advice.advice: Incorporate intermittent fasting into your routine.
actionable_advice.how_to: Set aside a period of time each day (e.g., 14-16 hours) where you don't eat to allow insulin levels to fall and your body to burn fat.

actionable_advice.advice: Reduce snacking and extend the time between meals.
actionable_advice.how_to: Aim for 3 meals a day and avoid eating between meals to keep insulin levels low.

actionable_advice.advice: Prioritize the hormonal impact of foods over calorie counting.
actionable_advice.how_to: Focus on consuming foods that don't cause rapid insulin spikes, such as whole, unprocessed foods, and be mindful of carbohydrate intake.

memorable_stories: Anecdote about a friend who got a six-pack eating pizza by strictly adhering to 'calories in, calories out', but then experienced a yo-yo effect after stopping the diet.
Personal story of colleagues who fasted during medical training and experienced no negative effects, contrasting with the public perception of fasting as harmful.
contrarian_claims: The idea that eating breakfast is essential for health is false for most people.
The 'calories in, calories out' model is an inadequate explanation of weight loss and weight gain.
Exercise is not very effective for weight loss, mainly because the amount of calories you burn doing exercise is just not very high.
Fasting increases, rather than decreases, your basal metabolic rate.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - Most Replayed Moment: Calories In, Calories Out Is A Myth! Why Most Diets Fail - Dr. Jason Fung
